hero.title: 🧠 The Medulla Oblongata
hero.subtitle: Cranial Nerve Nuclei & Brainstem Stroke Syndromes

sections.title: Core Anatomy & Landmarks
sections.hint: The Medulla is the 'Bulb' of the brainstem, extending from the pons to the foramen magnum.

topics.content:
• Pyramids: Ventral swellings containing corticospinal fibers. 90% cross at the Pyramidal Decussation (Lower Medulla).
  • Olives: Oval elevations lateral to pyramids containing the Inferior Olivary Nucleus (Cerebellar motor learning).
  • Sulci: CN XII exits the Anterolateral sulcus; CN IX, X, and XI exit the Posteralateral sulcus.

• Nucleus Ambiguus: Motor to muscles of larynx/pharynx (CN IX, X). Damage = Dysphagia/Hoarseness.
  • Solitary Nucleus: Sensory (Taste/Visceral). CN VII, IX, X.
  • Area Postrema: The 'Vomiting Center' located in the floor of the 4th ventricle.
  • Hypoglossal Nucleus: Motor to the tongue.

Vessel: Anterior Spinal Artery (ASA).

  • Ipsilateral: Hypoglossal palsy (Tongue deviates to lesion, atrophy).
  • Contralateral: Hemiparesis (Pyramid) and loss of Fine Touch/Proprioception (Medial Lemniscus).

Vessel: PICA (Posterior Inferior Cerebellar Artery).

  • Ipsilateral Face: Loss of pain/temp (Trigeminal nucleus).
  • Contralateral Body: Loss of pain/temp (Spinothalamic tract).
  • Autonomics: Ipsilateral Horner’s Syndrome (Ptosis, Miosis, Anhydrosis).
  • CN IX/X: Hoarseness, dysphagia, loss of gag reflex (Nucleus Ambiguus).

medulla.q: Between which two structures does CN XII emerge?
medulla.l: CN XII exits the ventrolateral sulcus between the pyramid and the olive.

medulla.q: Occlusion of the ASA (Anterior Spinal Artery) results in:
medulla.l: ASA supplies the medial medulla; PICA supplies the lateral medulla.

medulla.q: Ipsilateral tongue deviation with contralateral hemiparesis indicates damage to:
medulla.l: This is Medial Medullary Syndrome (Dejerine Syndrome).

medulla.q: Which nucleus is responsible for the hoarseness seen in Wallenberg Syndrome?
medulla.l: The Nucleus Ambiguus provides motor fibers to the larynx via CN X.

medulla.q: Which tract decussates in the lower medulla?
medulla.l: The pyramidal decussation is where 90% of motor fibers cross.

medulla.q: PICA supplies which part of the medulla?
medulla.l: PICA occlusion causes Lateral Medullary Syndrome.

medulla.q: Horner's syndrome in a medullary stroke is caused by damage to:
medulla.l: Disruption of descending sympathetic fibers in the lateral medulla causes Horner’s.

medulla.q: The 'Vomiting Center' is also known as the:
medulla.l: Area Postrema in the 4th ventricle floor triggers emesis.

medulla.q: Loss of pain/temp on the ipsilateral face is due to damage of:
medulla.l: Spinal Trigeminal nucleus handles ipsilateral facial pain/temp.

medulla.q: Damage to the Medial Lemniscus results in contralateral loss of:
medulla.l: Medial Lemniscus carries dorsal column information.

medulla.q: Where is the respiratory rhythmicity center located?
medulla.l: The medulla controls the basic rhythm of breathing.

medulla.q: A patient cannot swallow and has no gag reflex. Which nucleus is likely damaged?
medulla.l: Nucleus Ambiguus controls the muscles for swallowing.

medulla.q: Proprioception from the lower limbs travels in which fasciculus?
medulla.l: Gracilis is for the lower body (T6 and below); Cuneatus is for the upper body.

medulla.q: The Inferior Olivary Nucleus sends fibers to the:
medulla.l: It provides climbing fibers to the cerebellum.

medulla.q: Which cranial nerves are associated with the 'Rule of 4' for the Medulla?
medulla.l: CN 9-12 are the medullary cranial nerves.

medulla.q: A lesion in the right pyramid will cause weakness on which side?
medulla.l: Since fibers cross below the pyramids, the deficit is contralateral.

medulla.q: Which structure forms the floor of the 4th ventricle in the medulla?
medulla.l: The rhomboid fossa forms the floor of the 4th ventricle.

medulla.q: Vertigo and Nystagmus in Wallenberg's are due to which nuclei?
medulla.l: Vestibular nuclei damage affects balance and eye coordination.

medulla.q: The pyramids are located on which aspect of the medulla?
medulla.l: Pyramids are ventral (anterior) landmarks.

medulla.q: The Spinal Trigeminal Nucleus extends from the pons down to the:
medulla.l: It is a long nucleus extending into the upper cervical spinal cord.

midbrain.q: Which cranial nerve emerges from the interpeduncular fossa of the midbrain?
midbrain.l: The oculomotor nerve (CN III) emerges anteriorly between the cerebral peduncles.

midbrain.q: A patient with Parinaud’s syndrome would most likely struggle with:
midbrain.l: Parinaud’s (Dorsal Midbrain Syndrome) classically causes paralysis of upward gaze.

midbrain.q: Weber’s Syndrome is characterized by:
a: Ipsilateral CN III palsy and contralateral hemiparesis
Contralateral CN III palsy and ipsilateral tremor
midbrain.l: Weber’s involves the ventral midbrain, affecting CN III and the pyramidal tract.

midbrain.q: Which structure is responsible for auditory reflexes?
midbrain.l: The Inferior Colliculus is a major relay in the auditory pathway.

midbrain.q: Decerebrate posturing indicates a lesion at what level?
midbrain.l: Decerebrate (extensor) posturing occurs when the lesion is in the brainstem below the red nucleus.

midbrain.q: Claude’s and Benedikt’s syndromes primarily involve damage to which nucleus?
midbrain.l: The Red Nucleus involvement causes the characteristic contralateral ataxia or tremor.

midbrain.q: Convergence-retraction nystagmus is a hallmark sign of:
midbrain.l: This is a classic feature of dorsal midbrain (Parinaud) lesions.

midbrain.q: Which artery provides the primary blood supply to the medial midbrain?
midbrain.l: Paramedian perforators supply the territory involved in Weber’s syndrome.

midbrain.q: A 'blown pupil' (fixed and dilated) in Weber’s syndrome is due to damage to:
midbrain.l: CN III carries parasympathetic fibers that constrict the pupil; damage causes dilation.

midbrain.q: Which structure connects the third and fourth ventricles?
midbrain.l: The Cerebral Aqueduct of Sylvius runs through the midbrain.

midbrain.q: The Superior Colliculus is primarily involved in:
midbrain.l: It helps coordinate head and eye movements in response to visual stimuli.

midbrain.q: Collier’s sign refers to which clinical finding?
midbrain.l: Collier’s sign (eyelid retraction) is seen in Parinaud Syndrome.

midbrain.q: Decorticate posturing is characterized by:
a: Flexion of arms and extension of legs
midbrain.l: Decorticate involves 'flexing to the core' (arms flexed, legs extended).

midbrain.q: Which midbrain syndrome is most likely caused by a Pineal gland tumor?
midbrain.l: Pineal tumors compress the dorsal midbrain (tectum), leading to Parinaud’s.

midbrain.q: If a patient has a right-sided midbrain lesion in the cerebral peduncle, the motor deficit will be on the:
midbrain.l: Corticospinal fibers cross in the medulla, so a midbrain lesion causes contralateral deficits.

midbrain.q: The Edinger-Westphal nucleus provides innervation for:
midbrain.l: It provides parasympathetic supply to the ciliary ganglion for miosis.

midbrain.q: Which midbrain structure produces Dopamine and is lost in Parkinson’s?
midbrain.l: The Substantia Nigra pars compacta contains dopaminergic neurons.

midbrain.q: The Trochlear nerve (CN IV) is unique because it:
a: Exits from the dorsal aspect of the brainstem
midbrain.l: CN IV is the only cranial nerve to emerge dorsally and the only one to cross completely.

midbrain.q: Light-near dissociation (Pseudo-Argyll Robertson pupil) is found in:
midbrain.l: This is a feature of Parinaud's where pupils react to accommodation but not light.

midbrain.q: Which tract is responsible for the 'Flexor' component of decorticate posturing?
midbrain.l: The Red Nucleus/Rubrospinal tract facilitates upper limb flexion.
